(abbreviated **rpm**, **RPM**, **rev/min**, **r/min**, or **r⋅min−1**) is a unit of [rotational speed](/wiki/Rotational_speed "Rotational speed") (or [rotational frequency](/wiki/Rotational_frequency "Rotational frequency")) for rotating machines.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Rpm](/w/index.php?title=Rpm&redirect=no "Rpm"))

Unit of rotational speed

"RPM" redirects here. For other uses, see [RPM (disambiguation)](/wiki/RPM_(disambiguation) "RPM (disambiguation)") and [revolutions per minute (disambiguation)](/wiki/Revolutions_per_minute_(disambiguation) "Revolutions per minute (disambiguation)").

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Revolutions_per_minute "Special:EditPage/Revolutions per minute") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Revolutions per minute"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Revolutions+per+minute%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Revolutions+per+minute%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Revolutions+per+minute%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Revolutions+per+minute%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Revolutions+per+minute%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Revolutions+per+minute%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(July 2016)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

**Revolutions per minute** (abbreviated **rpm**, **RPM**, **rev/min**, **r/min**, or **r⋅min−1**) is a unit of [rotational speed](/wiki/Rotational_speed "Rotational speed") (or [rotational frequency](/wiki/Rotational_frequency "Rotational frequency")) for rotating machines.
One revolution per [minute](/wiki/Minute "Minute") is equivalent to ⁠1/60⁠ [hertz](/wiki/Hertz "Hertz").

## Standards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Revolutions_per_minute&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Standards")]

[ISO 80000-3](/wiki/ISO_80000-3 "ISO 80000-3"):2019 defines a [physical quantity](/wiki/Physical_quantity "Physical quantity") called [*rotation*](/wiki/Rotation_(quantity) "Rotation (quantity)") (or *number of revolutions*), [dimensionless](/wiki/Dimensionless "Dimensionless"), whose [instantaneous rate of change](/wiki/Instantaneous_rate_of_change "Instantaneous rate of change") is called *[rotational frequency](/wiki/Rotational_frequency "Rotational frequency")* (or *rate of rotation*), with units of [reciprocal seconds](/wiki/Reciprocal_second "Reciprocal second") (s−1).[[1]](#cite_note-1)

A related but distinct quantity for describing rotation is *[angular frequency](/wiki/Angular_frequency "Angular frequency")* (or *angular speed*, the magnitude of [angular velocity](/wiki/Angular_velocity "Angular velocity")), for which the SI unit is the [radian per second](/wiki/Radian_per_second "Radian per second") (rad/s).

Although they have the same [dimensions](/wiki/Dimension_(physics) "Dimension (physics)") (reciprocal time) and base unit (s−1), the hertz (Hz) and radians per second (rad/s) are special names used to express two different but proportional [ISQ](/wiki/International_System_of_Quantities "International System of Quantities") quantities: frequency and angular frequency, respectively. The conversions between a frequency f and an angular frequency ω are
![{\displaystyle \omega =2\pi f,\quad f={\frac {\omega }{2\pi }}.}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/4fb2714beafbde927f93261d3fe35b7dfd04e79b)

Thus a disc rotating at 60 rpm is said to have an angular speed of 2*π* rad/s and a rotation frequency of 1 Hz.

The [International System of Units](/wiki/International_System_of_Units "International System of Units") (SI) does not recognize rpm as a unit. It defines units of [angular frequency](/wiki/Angular_frequency "Angular frequency") and [angular velocity](/wiki/Angular_velocity "Angular velocity") as rad s−1, and units of [frequency](/wiki/Frequency "Frequency") as [Hz](/wiki/Hertz "Hertz"), equal to s−1.

![{\displaystyle {\begin{array}{rcrcr}1~{\dfrac {\text{rad}}{\text{s}}}&=&{\dfrac {1}{2\pi }}~{\text{Hz}}&=&{\dfrac {60}{2\pi }}~{\text{rpm}}\\2\pi ~{\dfrac {\text{rad}}{\text{s}}}&=&1~{\text{Hz}}&=&60~{\text{rpm}}\\{\dfrac {2\pi }{60}}~{\dfrac {\text{rad}}{\text{s}}}&=&{\dfrac {1}{60}}~{\text{Hz}}&=&1~{\text{rpm}}\end{array}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/8ae20224fbe2c340975d7270e6052182d5164079)

## Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Revolutions_per_minute&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Examples")]

* For a wheel, a pump, or a crank shaft, the number of times that it completes one full cycle in one minute is given the unit revolution per minute. A revolution is one complete period of motion, whether this be circular, reciprocating or some other periodic motion.
* On many kinds of disc recording media, the rotational speed of the medium under the read head is a standard given in rpm. [Phonograph (gramophone) records](/wiki/Phonograph_record "Phonograph record"), for example, typically rotate steadily at 16+2⁄3, 33+1⁄3, 45 rpm or 78 rpm (0.28, 0.55, 0.75, or 1.3, respectively, in Hz).
* Air turbine rotating up to 1500000 rpm (25 kHz)
* Modern air turbine [dental drills](/wiki/Dental_drill "Dental drill") can rotate at over 800000 rpm (13.3 kHz).
* The [second](/wiki/Second "Second") hand of a conventional analog clock rotates at 1 rpm.
* [Audio CD](/wiki/Red_Book_(audio_CD_standard) "Red Book (audio CD standard)") players read their discs at a precise, constant rate (4.3218 Mbit/s of raw physical data for 1.4112 Mbit/s (176.4 KB/s) of usable audio data) and thus must vary the disc's rotational speed from 8 Hz (480 rpm) when reading at the innermost edge to 3.5 Hz (210 rpm) at the outer edge.[[2]](#cite_note-MPEG_DVD_Spec-2)
* [DVD](/wiki/DVD "DVD") players also usually read discs at a constant linear rate. The disc's rotational speed varies from 25.5 Hz (1530 rpm) when reading at the innermost edge, to 10.5 Hz (630 rpm) at the outer edge.[[2]](#cite_note-MPEG_DVD_Spec-2)
* A [washing machine](/wiki/Washing_machine "Washing machine")'s drum may rotate at 500 rpm to 2763 rpm (8 Hz – 46 Hz) during the spin cycles.
* A [baseball](/wiki/Baseball "Baseball") thrown by a [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") pitcher can rotate at over 2500 rpm (41.7 Hz); faster rotation yields more movement on [breaking balls](/wiki/Breaking_ball "Breaking ball").[[3]](#cite_note-3)
* A power-generation turbine ([with a two-pole alternator](/wiki/Alternator#Synchronous_speeds "Alternator")) rotates at 3000 rpm (50 Hz), 3600 rpm (60 Hz), and over 4000 rpm (66+2⁄3 Hz)
* Modern [automobile](/wiki/Automobile "Automobile") [engines](/wiki/Engine "Engine") are typically operated around 1600 rpm – 2800 rpm (31 Hz – 48 Hz) when cruising, with a minimum (idle) speed around 750 rpm – 900 rpm (12.5 Hz – 15 Hz), and an upper limit anywhere from 4800 rpm to up to 9500 rpm (80 Hz – 158 Hz) for a road car, very rarely reaching up to 10000 rpm for certain cars (such as the [GMA T.50](/wiki/GMA_T.50 "GMA T.50")), or 22000 rpm for racing engines such as [those in Formula 1 cars](/wiki/Formula_One_engines "Formula One engines") (during the [2006](/wiki/2006_Formula_One_World_Championship "2006 Formula One World Championship") season, with the 2.4 L N/A [V8 engine](/wiki/V8_engine "V8 engine") configuration; limited to 15000 rpm, with the 1.6 L [V6](/wiki/V6_engine "V6 engine") [turbo](/wiki/Turbocharger "Turbocharger")-[hybrid](/wiki/Hybrid_electric_vehicle "Hybrid electric vehicle") engine configuration).[[4]](#cite_note-4) The exhaust note of [V8](/wiki/V8_engine "V8 engine"), [V10](/wiki/V10_engine "V10 engine"), and [V12](/wiki/V12_engine "V12 engine") [F1 cars](/wiki/F1_car "F1 car") has a much higher pitch than an [I4 engine](/wiki/Straight_engine "Straight engine"), because each of the [cylinders](/wiki/Cylinder_(engine) "Cylinder (engine)") of a [four-stroke engine](/wiki/Four-stroke_engine "Four-stroke engine") fires once for every two revolutions of the [crankshaft](/wiki/Crankshaft "Crankshaft"). Thus an eight-cylinder engine turning 300 times per second will have an exhaust note of 1200 Hz.
* Large [two-stroke](/wiki/Two-stroke_engine "Two-stroke engine") slow speed [diesel engines](/wiki/Diesel_engine "Diesel engine") are often used as [ship engines](/wiki/Marine_propulsion#Diesel "Marine propulsion"). Most of them operate below 120 rpm, and some very long stroke engines have a maximum speed of around 80 rpm.
* A piston [aircraft engine](/wiki/Aircraft_engine "Aircraft engine") typically rotates at a rate between 2500 rpm and 10000 rpm (42 Hz – 166 Hz).
* Computer [hard drives](/wiki/Hard_drive "Hard drive") typically rotate at 7500 rpm – 10000 rpm (125 Hz – 166 Hz), the most common speeds for the [ATA](/wiki/Advanced_Technology_Attachment "Advanced Technology Attachment") or [SATA](/wiki/Serial_ATA "Serial ATA")-based drives in consumer models. High-performance drives (used in fileservers and enthusiast-gaming PCs) rotate at 10000 rpm – 15000 rpm (160 Hz – 250 Hz), usually with higher-level SATA, [SCSI](/wiki/SCSI "SCSI") or [Fibre Channel](/wiki/Fibre_Channel "Fibre Channel") interfaces and smaller platters to allow these higher speeds, the reduction in storage capacity and ultimate outer-edge speed paying off in much quicker access time and average transfer speed thanks to the high spin rate. Until recently, lower-end and power-efficient laptop drives could be found with 4200 rpm or even 3600 rpm spindle speeds (70 Hz or 60 Hz), but these have fallen out of favour due to their lower performance, improvements in energy efficiency in faster models and the takeup of [solid-state drives](/wiki/Solid-state_drive "Solid-state drive") for use in slimline and ultraportable laptops. Similar to CD and DVD media, the amount of data that can be stored or read for each turn of the disc is greater at the outer edge than near the spindle; however, hard drives keep a constant rotational speed so the effective data rate is faster at the edge (conventionally, the "start" of the disc, opposite to a CD or DVD).
* [Floppy disc](/wiki/Floppy_disc "Floppy disc") drives typically ran at a constant 300 rpm or occasionally 360 rpm (a relatively slow 5 Hz or 6 Hz) with a constant per-revolution data density, which was simple and inexpensive to implement, though inefficient. Some designs such as those used with older Apple computers (Lisa, early Macintosh, later II's) were more complex and used variable rotational speeds and per-track storage density (at a constant read/record rate) to store more data per disc; for example, between 394 rpm (with 12 sectors per track) and 590 rpm (8 sectors) with Mac's 800 kB double-density drive at a constant 39.4 kB/s (max) – versus 300 rpm, 720 kB and 23 kB/s (max) for double-density drives in other machines.[[5]](#cite_note-5)
* A [Zippe-type centrifuge](/wiki/Zippe-type_centrifuge "Zippe-type centrifuge") for enriching uranium spins at 100000 rpm (1666 Hz) or faster.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
* [Gas turbine](/wiki/Gas_turbine "Gas turbine") engines rotate at tens of thousands of rpm. JetCat model aircraft turbines are capable of over 100000 rpm (1700 Hz) with the fastest reaching 165780 rpm (2763 Hz).[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* A [flywheel energy storage](/wiki/Flywheel_energy_storage "Flywheel energy storage") system works at 60000 rpm – 500000 rpm (1 kHz – 8.3 kHz) range using a passively magnetic levitated flywheel in a vacuum.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The choice of the flywheel material is not the most dense, but the one that pulverises the most safely, at surface speeds about 7 times the speed of sound.
* A typical 80 mm, 30 CFM computer fan will spin at 2600 rpm – 3000 rpm (43 Hz – 50 Hz) on 12 V DC power.
* A [millisecond pulsar](/wiki/Millisecond_pulsar "Millisecond pulsar") can have near 50000 rpm (833 Hz).
* A [turbocharger](/wiki/Turbocharger "Turbocharger") can reach 1000000 rpm (16.6 kHz), while 100000 rpm – 250000 rpm (1 kHz – 3 kHz) is common.
* A [supercharger](/wiki/Supercharger "Supercharger") can spin at speeds between or as high as 50000 rpm – 100000 rpm (833 Hz – 1666 Hz)
* [Molecular microbiology](/wiki/Molecular_biology "Molecular biology") – molecular engines. The rotation rates of bacterial [flagella](/wiki/Flagellum "Flagellum") have been measured to be 10200 rpm (170 Hz) for *[Salmonella typhimurium](/wiki/Salmonella_enterica_subsp._enterica "Salmonella enterica subsp. enterica")*, 16200 rpm (270 Hz) for *[Escherichia coli](/wiki/Escherichia_coli "Escherichia coli")*, and up to 500000 rpm (1700 Hz) for polar flagellum of *[Vibrio alginolyticus](/wiki/Vibrio_alginolyticus "Vibrio alginolyticus")*, allowing the latter organism to move in simulated natural conditions at a maximum speed of 540 mm/h.[[9]](#cite_note-9)
* The sample in [magic angle spinning](/wiki/Magic_angle_spinning "Magic angle spinning"), a [nuclear magnetic resonance](/wiki/Nuclear_magnetic_resonance "Nuclear magnetic resonance") technique, typically rotates between 300000 and 6000000 rpm (5–100 kHz), with experimental instruments reaching speeds as high as 12000000 rpm (200 kHz).
